### Source A 
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/2fbbec29-f7c8-45dd-a5e1-45fe9877397f)

### Source B
It is a paradox to brand a technological innovation as tainted goods by its very name. ‘Deepfake’ is a victim of its own capabilities. Negative connotations and recent incidents have pigeonholed this innovation in the taboo zone. The rise of deepfake technology has ushered in very interesting possibilities and challenges. This synthetic media, created through sophisticated artificial intelligence algorithms, has begun to infiltrate various sectors, raising intriguing questions about its potential impact on education and employability.

The dawn of deepfake technology introduces a realm of possibilities in education. Imagine medical students engaging in lifelike surgical simulations or language learners participating in authentic conversations. The potential for deepfake to revolutionise training scenarios is vast and could significantly enhance the educational experience. Beyond simulations, deepfake can transport students to historical events through realistic reenactments or facilitate virtual field trips, transcending the boundaries of traditional education. The immersive nature of deepfake content holds the promise of making learning more engaging and memorable.




### Source C 
However, while these potential abuses of the technology are real and concerning that doesn't mean we should turn a blind eye to the technology’s potential when using it responsibly, says Jaime Donally, a well-known immersive learning expert. 

“Typically, when we're hearing about it, it's in terms of the negative – impersonation and giving false claims,” Donally says. “But really, the technology has the power of bringing people from history alive through old images that we have using AI.” 

Donally, a former math teacher and instructional technologist, has written about how a type of deep fake technology called deep nostalgia technology that went viral in 2021 can allow students to form a stronger connection with the past and their personal family heritage. The technology, available on the MyHeritage app, allows images to uploaded that are then turned into short animations thanks to AI technology. 

Here are some of the ways in which teachers can utilize deep fake technology in the classroom utilizing the MyHeritage app.
Teachers have used the deep fake technology in the My Heritage app to bring historical figures such as Amelia Earhart and Albert Einstein to life. One teacher Donally has communicated with used an animation of Frederick Douglass (above) to help students connect with Douglass’ famous 1852 speech about the meaning of the Fourth of July to Black enslaved Americans. Another teacher has plans to use the app to have students interview a historical figure and create dialogue for them, then match the dialogue to the animation.

Donally herself has paired animations she's created with other types of immersive technology. “I layered it on top in augmented reality,” she says. “When you scan the photo of my grandfather, it all came to life. And it became something that was much more relevant to see in your real-world space.” 

With proper supervision, students can use the technology to animate images of family members or local historical figures, and can experiment with augmented reality (AR) in the process. 
“It makes you want to learn more,” Donally says of animations created using deep fake technology. “It drives you into kind of the history and understanding a bit more, and I think it also helps you identify who you are in that process.” 

### Source D 
Education platforms are harnessing deepfake technology to create AI tutors that provide customised support to students. Rather than a generic video lecture, each learner can get tailored instruction and feedback from a virtual tutor who speaks their language and adjusts to their level.

For example, Anthropic built Claude, an AI assistant designed specifically for education. Claude can answer students’ natural language questions, explain concepts clearly, and identify knowledge gaps.

Such AI tutors make learning more effective, accessible, and inclusive. Students feel like they have an expert guide helping them master new skills and material.

AI and deepfake technology have enormous potential to enhance workforce training and education in immersive new ways too.
As Drew Rose, CSO and founder of cybersecurity firm Living Security, explains, “educators can leverage deepfakes to create immersive learning experiences. For instance, a history lesson might feature a ‘guest appearance’ by a historical figure, or a science lesson might have a renowned scientist explaining complex concepts.”
Ivana Bartoletti, privacy and data protection expert at Wipro and author of An Artificial Revolution – On Power, Politics and AI envisions similar applications.

“Deepfake technologies could provide an easier and less expensive way to train and visualise,” she says.
“Students of medicine and nursing currently train with animatronic robots. They are expensive and require special control rooms. Generative AI and augmented or virtual reality headsets or practice rooms will be cheaper and allow for the generalisation, if not the gamification, of simulation.”

Medical students could gain experience diagnosing and treating simulated patients, while business students could practice high-stakes scenarios like negotiations without real-world consequences.
These immersive, gamified environments enabled by AI and deepfakes also have vast potential for corporate training.

Bartoletti notes, “A similar use case could be made for other types of learning that require risky and skill-based experiences. The Air Force uses AI as adversaries in flight simulators, and humans have not beaten the best AIs since 2015.

questions.specification:
**Source A**  

![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/29a1e05c-145a-4014-9b07-d7fd4d0914db)


**Source B (MetroStream website excerpt)**  


MetroStream is Harbor City’s publicly funded streaming channel, created when the city’s local broadcaster merged its TV newsroom with its radio team and a small digital studio. MetroStream publishes each major story in several formats: short vertical clips optimized for phones, longer segments for the streaming channel, and audio recaps for commuters. The service also runs interactive ads in the mobile app, where viewers can tap to save a coupon or follow a sponsor link. MetroStream states that sponsors do not choose which stories appear, but the app prioritizes “viewer-friendly” formats such as short clips and captioned highlights. For transparency, MetroStream labels sponsored segments and keeps an archive of full-length interviews to “reduce selective quoting.” Critics argue that the shift toward short clips changes what counts as news.  


**Source C (local newspaper report statistics)**  


In 2026, MetroStream published 2.8× more items than the old broadcaster, largely due to short clips and audio recaps.  

Average watch time: vertical clips 19 seconds, long segments 6.5 minutes.  
62% of mobile viewers watch with captions on; 18% of viewers report they “rarely” open the full interview archive.  

Sponsored segments account for 14% of total mobile views but generate 53% of mobile revenue.  

Complaints about “misleading context” rose by 27% after the app introduced auto-generated highlight reels.  


**Source D (same as above)**  


MetroStream calls itself “modern public media,” but it is drifting toward the logic of platforms: compress the story, maximize the scroll, and monetize attention through interactivity. The newsroom may be converged, yet the audience experience is fragmented. One group gets 19-second clips with captions, another gets long segments, and almost nobody checks the interview archive that is supposed to provide accountability. Sponsored segments are labelled, yes, but the deeper influence is structural: when half your mobile revenue comes from a small portion of ad-friendly content, the system quietly teaches editors what to prioritize. Public media is meant to create shared understanding, not just shared metrics. The danger is not only misinformation; it is “thin information”; that is, news that is technically accurate but stripped of context, uncertainty, and depth. MetroStream should not confuse multi-format distribution with democratic communication.

parts.content: Describe how Source A shows MetroStream using multiple media formats to distribute a single story.
parts.markscheme:
**Describe (Total <marks>2 marks</marks>)**

  
**Answer points**

  
* Source A shows a single story presented as a short vertical video clip on a phone screen <marks>1 mark</marks>  
* The same story appears as a longer streaming segment on a larger screen or broadcast format <marks>1 mark</marks>  
* An audio recap option is shown as a third format for the same news story <marks>1 mark</marks>  
* The story content is adapted across different delivery platforms including mobile, streaming, and broadcast <marks>1 mark</marks>  

**Awarding marks**

  
Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for identifying one media format shown in Source A.  
Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for identifying a second distinct format and indicating it represents the same story repackaged across platforms.  

**Marking guidance**

  
<note>The response must clearly identify formats visible in Source A and show they are the same story distributed differently. Merely listing formats without linking them to a single story limits the response to <marks>1 mark</marks>.</note>  

**Sample answer**

  
Source A shows MetroStream distributing one story across multiple media formats. The story is presented as a short vertical video clip designed for phone viewing and also appears as a longer streaming segment on a larger screen.  

  
An audio recap provides a third format of the same content. This demonstrates how MetroStream repackages the same news story across different platforms suited to mobile, streaming, and broadcast audiences.

parts.content: Explain how MetroStream’s use of interactive ads and short clips in Source B could influence what audiences understand as “news.”
parts.markscheme:
**Explain (Total <marks>4 marks</marks>)**

  
**Answer points**

  
* Short clips and prioritisation of viewer friendly formats can encourage audiences to treat highlights as complete news stories rather than summaries <marks>1 mark</marks>  
* The emphasis on brief vertical content reduces depth and context, changing expectations of what counts as substantial news <marks>1 mark</marks>  
* Interactive ads and sponsor links blend content creation with monetisation, reinforcing news as something to tap, consume, or scroll through quickly <marks>1 mark</marks>  
* The attention driven design shifts audience norms toward viewing news through engagement metrics rather than informational value <marks>1 mark</marks>  
* Even with sponsorship labels and archive links, most user exposure comes through compressed formats that emphasise brevity over nuance <marks>1 mark</marks>  
* The structural influence of revenue dependence on ad friendly content shapes editorial choices about what gets prioritised as news <marks>1 mark</marks>  

**Awarding marks**

  
Award up to <marks>2 marks</marks> for each explained effect where the response links a specific feature to a consequence for audience understanding of news.  
To earn <marks>2 marks</marks> for an effect, the answer must include both the feature and its consequence for audience behaviour or understanding.  

**Marking guidance**

  
<note>Responses that only list features or only describe consequences without connecting them can be awarded a maximum of <marks>2 marks</marks> Full marks require clear cause and effect explanations showing how technical features influence audience perceptions of news.</note>  

**Sample answer**

  
MetroStream’s use of short clips and viewer friendly prioritisation can influence audiences to treat brief highlights as complete news, reducing depth and context in what they expect from journalism. Interactive ads and sponsor links blend news content with commercial incentives, encouraging audiences to view news as something to tap or scroll through rather than fully absorb.  

  
This attention driven design shifts norms toward engagement metrics over information value. Even though sponsorship labels and archives exist, most exposure happens through compressed formats that emphasise speed and brevity, potentially reshaping what audiences understand news to be.

parts.content: Compare what Source C and Source D suggest about the impact of MetroStream’s mobile-first approach on trust and context.
parts.markscheme:
**Compare (Total <marks>6 marks</marks>)**

  
**Answer points**

  
* Source C provides statistical evidence of short attention spans, with average watch times of nineteen seconds for vertical clips compared to six point five minutes for long segments <marks>1 mark</marks>  
* Source D interprets this short attention pattern as creating fragmented audiences where different groups receive different levels of detail from the same news <marks>1 mark</marks>  
* Source C reports that eighteen percent of viewers rarely open the full interview archive, showing limited use of the transparency mechanism <marks>1 mark</marks>  
* Source D argues that this underuse of archives undermines accountability because almost nobody accesses the full context that is supposed to provide checks <marks>1 mark</marks>  
* Source C shows sponsored segments account for fourteen percent of views but generate fifty three percent of mobile revenue, creating financial dependence on ad friendly content <marks>1 mark</marks>  
* Source D explains this revenue dependence structurally influences editorial priorities because systems teach editors what to prioritise based on financial incentives <marks>1 mark</marks>  
* Source C records a twenty seven percent rise in misleading context complaints after auto generated highlight reels were introduced <marks>1 mark</marks>  
* Source D connects this to the concept of thin information, which is technically accurate but stripped of uncertainty, context, and depth <marks>1 mark</marks>  

**Awarding marks**

  
Award <marks>2 marks</marks> per effective comparison that explicitly references both Source C and Source D in the same point.  
Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for referencing one source only or for vague comparisons that do not clearly link both sources to a specific impact on trust or context.  

**Marking guidance**

  
<note>Responses that analyse the sources separately without connecting them can be awarded a maximum of <marks>3 marks</marks>. To reach <marks>6 marks</marks>, the response must consistently draw comparisons between what Source C presents as evidence and what Source D offers as interpretation or implication.</note>  

**Sample answer**

  
Source C provides statistical evidence of short attention spans, showing that vertical clips average nineteen seconds while long segments average six point five minutes, which Source D interprets as creating fragmented audiences where different groups receive news at different depths. Source C reports that only a small portion of viewers regularly access the full interview archive, and Source D argues this weakens accountability because the transparency mechanism intended to provide context goes largely unused.  

  
While Source C shows that sponsored segments generate fifty three percent of mobile revenue despite accounting for only fourteen percent of views, Source D explains how this structural dependence on ad friendly content teaches editors what to prioritise. Source C also records a twenty seven percent increase in misleading context complaints, and Source D connects this to the problem of thin information, where news remains technically accurate but loses uncertainty, context, and depth. Both sources suggest that MetroStream’s mobile first approach creates challenges for trust and contextual understanding.

parts.content: Discuss whether MetroStream demonstrates the benefits or the problems of digital media convergence. Answer this with reference to all the sources (A–D) and your own knowledge of the Digital Society course. Consider how media are created and distributed across platforms, the role of advertising and attention, and the ethical dilemmas of compression, framing, and sponsorship.
parts.markscheme:
**Discuss (Total <marks>12 marks</marks>)**

  
**Answer points**

  
* Convergence increases output and accessibility by enabling multi format distribution that serves different audience preferences and contexts <marks>1 mark</marks>  
* Source B and Source C show MetroStream publishing two point eight times more items than the old broadcaster through vertical clips, audio recaps, and captioned highlights <marks>1 mark</marks>  
* Public media convergence can attempt transparency measures such as sponsorship labels and full interview archives to maintain accountability <marks>1 mark</marks>  
* Compression and highlights can decontextualise news by presenting fragments as complete stories, reducing depth and nuance <marks>1 mark</marks>  
* Source C shows complaints about misleading context rising by twenty seven percent after auto generated highlight reels were introduced <marks>1 mark</marks>  
* Source D argues this creates thin information, which is technically accurate but stripped of context, uncertainty, and proper framing <marks>1 mark</marks>  
* Attention economics in digital convergence can shape editorial priorities when revenue depends heavily on ad friendly formats <marks>1 mark</marks>  
* Source C demonstrates that sponsored segments generate most mobile revenue from a small portion of views, creating financial incentives that influence what gets prioritised <marks>1 mark</marks>  
* Fragmented audience experiences can undermine the shared public sphere that public media is meant to create <marks>1 mark</marks>  
* Own knowledge examples include platformisation, algorithmic curation, framing effects, native advertising ethics, and context collapse in news consumption <marks>1 mark</marks>  
* Ethical dilemmas arise between democratic access to information and attention driven distortion that prioritises engagement over understanding <marks>1 mark</marks>  
* Strong responses may suggest safeguards such as editorial firewalls, context prompts, or better archive integration to balance benefits and risks <marks>1 mark</marks>  

**Awarding marks**

  
Award up to <marks>6 marks</marks> for discussing benefits of digital media convergence with reference to the sources.  
Award up to <marks>6 marks</marks> for discussing problems of digital media convergence with reference to the sources.  
Award additional marks for own knowledge of Digital Society concepts such as platformisation, algorithmic curation, framing effects, or native advertising ethics.  

**Marking guidance**

  
<note>Responses that focus only on benefits or only on problems are limited in depth regardless of detail. To reach <marks>12 marks</marks>, the response must present a balanced argument, reference all four sources, and integrate relevant own knowledge examples from the Digital Society course. Answers using only source material or only own knowledge can be awarded a maximum of <marks>8 marks</marks>.</note>  

**Sample answer**

  
MetroStream demonstrates both benefits and problems of digital media convergence. Convergence increases output and accessibility by enabling multi format distribution that serves different audiences. Source B and Source C show MetroStream publishing two point eight times more items than the old broadcaster through vertical clips, audio recaps, and captioned highlights. This expanded reach represents a benefit of convergent media systems, alongside transparency measures such as sponsorship labels and full interview archives.  

  
However, compression and highlights can decontextualise news. Source C records a twenty seven percent increase in misleading context complaints after auto generated highlight reels were introduced, and Source D argues this creates thin information that is technically accurate but stripped of context, uncertainty, and depth. Attention economics also create problems when revenue dependence shapes editorial priorities. Source C shows sponsored segments generate most mobile revenue despite accounting for few views, and Source D explains how this structural influence teaches editors what to prioritise.  

  
From Digital Society theory, these issues link to platformisation, algorithmic curation, framing effects, native advertising ethics, and context collapse. While MetroStream improves access and output, the pressures of advertising, compression, and fragmented attention risk undermining the depth and shared understanding expected of public media. Safeguards such as editorial firewalls, context prompts, and stronger archive integration could help balance these competing demands.

questions.specification:
**Fake news**

We see and hear news every day and trust that the information provided is accurate. That belief may soon end.

Artificial intelligence (AI) software is now being developed that can produce fake video footage of public figures using recordings of their own voices. Using as little as one minute of user-generated content (data), it can reproduce a particular person’s voice. The developer of this software demonstrated the results by using the voices of Bill Clinton, George Bush and Barack Obama in a computer-generated conversation.

Once a person’s voice has been reproduced, a fake video can be created by processing hundreds of videos of the person’s face. Video footage of politicians are often used, as there is so much data available online.

Law professor John Silverman commented that, as humans we tend to believe what we see, and the increased number of tools to make fake media that is unrecognizable from real media is going to prove a major challenge in the future.

parts.content: Discuss the claim that companies who develop software that can create fake videos of politicians should be accountable for the fake videos posted by users of their software on social media platforms.
parts.markscheme:
_Answers may include:_

**Software companies should be accountable (claim)**

*   Although there may be no legal requirement for the software company to monitor the videos of users of their software, there may be ethical reasons why they should, and it is not appropriate to hide behind an end-user agreement when the software has the potential to develop fake videos (ethics, values, transparency).
*   The software company has not been seen to take all avoidable steps to prevent the fake videos being posted / the software company can be proved to be acting outside the spirit of the law or maliciously (ethics, values).
*   The software company has allowed users to purchase the software in countries where there may be rigorous censorship laws appreciating that its use may be seen as unlawful (ethics, values).

**Software companies should not be accountable (counter-claim)**

*   If the software company has positioned itself as a responsible developer, and their policy documentation explicitly shows that they are practising what they preach (values, ethics) and have acted responsibly by minimizing the potential harm that may be caused.
*   It is unrealistic and unenforceable, however well intentioned, for the software company to be accountable for the content of the videos.
*   If the end-user agreement stated explicitly that the user would be accountable, would that clause be enforceable by the software company?
*   It is hard to determine at what point the software company would be accountable, as the software itself does not have the capability to cause harm, it is the user whodoes so (values).
*   At what point is a video considered fake? Is a spoof video a fake video (media authenticity)?
*   What happened to free speech or freedom of expression (values, ethics)?

_In this question it is expected there will be a balance between the terminology related to digital systems and the terminology related to social and ethical impacts._

**Keywords:** politics, political speech, lobbying, machine learning, speech recognition, image analysis, deepfakes, media authenticity, synthetic digital media, monitoring, accountability, responsibility, change, expression, power, values, ethics

parts.content:
**Should we completely automate journalism?**

Some of the news articles that you read are written by automated journalism software. This software uses algorithms and natural language generators to turn facts and trends into news stories.

_Narrative Science_, a company that produces automated journalism software, predicts that by 2026 up to 90 % of news articles could be generated by machine learning algorithms.

Discuss whether it is acceptable for news articles to be generated by automated journalism software.
parts.markscheme:
_Answers may include:_

**Reasons why automated journalism software is acceptable (claim):**

*   Automation leads to articles being generated faster – this is important for breaking news (systems).
*   It allows papers to reach a lot of niche audiences therefore increasing its reach.
*   It can give reporters more time to do in-depth analysis of the articles they write.
*   It can free up reporters for stories that require human thought / can eliminate some of the more mundane/boring jobs and allow reporters to focus on more thought-provoking stories.
*   It cannot deal with subjective material, so reporters’ jobs are not at risk (values).

**Reasons why automated journalism software is not acceptable (counter-claim):**

*   It takes time and money to train the machine learning algorithms (systems).
*   Robotic journalism can provide statistics but not analysis.
*   Robotic journalism can’t provide the human perspective that makes articles interesting / may have inherent biases (values).
*   Robotic journalism can create and promulgate “fake news”, as they will not know whether a story is true or not.
*   Robotic journalism can’t discover news stories; they are restricted to information available online / the accuracy of the underlying data still matters.
*   Robotic journalism is designed by humans who make mistakes, so robots can make mistakes too. Who is accountable for these mistakes? What regulations are in force? Where does power lie?
*   Some newspapers do not disclose what they are doing (lack of transparency).
*   Data will have to be constantly checked to make sure it is up to date.
*   Journalists, especially inexperienced ones who are still learning. will lose jobs as a result of the automation of the process.

_  
Note to examiners – accept terms such as automated journalism_, _algorithmic journalism or robotic journalism._

_In this question it is expected there will be a balance between the terminology related to digital systems and the terminology related to social and ethical impacts._

  
**Keywords:** economic, regulations, codes, policies, transparency, accountability, bias, perspective, automation, machine learning, algorithm, change, power, systems, values

questions.specification:
**Voice simulation**

Lyrebird voice-cloning software was created by three university students from Montreal. This technology was originally developed to change the lives of people who have lost their voice. These people can use the software to create a new voice by choosing one from samples provided in the software.

However, Lyrebird can also clone voices. Lyrebird claims that it can clone a person’s voice in just 60 seconds. Users only need to access the website, create an account, and generate a voice print by repeating a number of predetermined sentences. Although the cloning is not perfect, the developers of Lyrebird believe that both the time to clone the voice and the quality of the cloning will improve in the future.

The software allows the user to alter the speech and add emotion (such as, anger, elation, and surprise), increasing the ways in which it can be used.

The initial voice print for the cloning process requires the most time and a lot of processing power. However, creating speech from the voice print is a simple process, generating around 1000 sentences almost instantly. The voice print is saved as a compressed audio file.

Lyrebird has considered the ethical issues regarding biometrics\* and acknowledges that it highlights some key societal issues. Lyrebird has taken the decision to make the software available to all in an attempt to address these issues.  
  

* * *

\* biometrics: a means of personal identification through analysis of unique physical traits or behavioural characteristics (for example, through voice patterns or fingerprints)

parts.content: To what extent do the benefits of using Lyrebird’s voice-cloning software outweigh the concerns that may arise from its use?
parts.markscheme:
_Answers may include:_

**Benefits may include:**

*   It could help people who have lost their voice.
*   It could create better voice-activated interfaces (systems).
*   Characters in video games could have individual voices or could clone a player’s voice as a playable character (expression, identity).
*   It could provide interactive training and learning.
*   It could be used in educational software to produce a more realistic experience (systems).
*   Any device that requires speech could be enhanced by more natural voices (appointment reminders, personal assistant applications, audiobooks, etc.) (systems).
*   Parents could use the software to “read” to their children (relationships).

**Concerns may include:**

*   It could be used to create fake news or weaken the public’s confidence/trust in news of all kinds.
*   An increase in robocalls with realistic voices – scammers will be able to deceive people more easily or make fake calls to the police (values).
*   It may lead to systems that use voice as a method of authentication becoming compromised, as scammers are able to quickly create a user’s voice print and access the system.

_In part (c) of this question it is expected there will be a balance between the terminology related to digital systems and the terminology related to social and ethical impacts._

**Keywords:** health, privacy, anonymity, security, transparency, ownership, access, authentication, sharing, expression, identity, power, values

parts.content: Analysewhether it would be appropriate for Lyrebird to release the biometric data of a user to a third party.
parts.markscheme:
_Answers may include:_

**It would be appropriate:**

*   to law enforcement agencies for a warrant, subpoena, or court order
*   in response to requests by regulatory, legal or government agencies, as the information cannot be obtained by any other means
*   by withholding this information it would be working against the ethical principles of Lyrebird
*   if it is necessary or appropriate to do so to protect our rights, property, or safety
*   where the user has provided Lyrebird with written consent to disclosure of your biometric data.

**It would not be appropriate:**

*   where the releasing of the data would lead to harm being caused to the person whose data is being released, for example, data that is linked to an underlying medical condition (values, ethics)
*   where it is in direct contradiction of the policies that Lyrebird have for the sharing of data
*   as it could be harmful to the reputation of Lyrebird
*   as it is an invasion of privacy to release personal data without consent
*   it is illegal under the laws of the country in which Lyrebird operates (e.g., GDPR).

questions.specification:
### Source A: The Transformation of Journalism Through Digital Media
Digital media has disrupted traditional journalism, leading to audience fragmentation and the consolidation of news organizations. While digital platforms extend news reach, they also challenge revenue models and credibility.

For example, while Twitter provides instant news updates, it has also led to challenges in verifying information, as content can be quickly shared without fact-checking.


### Source B: Media Authenticity and Deepfakes
Deepfake technology, enabled by AI, allows for realistic but fabricated videos, raising concerns about authenticity and trust. The growing prevalence of deepfakes calls for new ways to verify digital content.

For example, deepfakes can create fabricated political speeches, potentially influencing public opinion through misinformation.


![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/526866e2-518b-4ead-a0f5-48d92d554307)

### Source C: Ownership, Copyright, and Remix Culture in Digital Media
Digital media has encouraged a remix culture, where creators can adapt existing content. However, this has brought up issues around copyright, ownership, and the need for Creative Commons licenses to legally share work.

For instance, remixes of popular songs or videos must comply with copyright laws, unless distributed under licenses that allow adaptations.

### Source D: Media Obsolescence and Digital Preservation
Digital content can quickly become obsolete, leading to media obsolescence and necessitating digital preservation. Websites and digital files often become inaccessible without updates or archiving efforts.

For example, digital archives like the Internet Archive preserve web content, ensuring continued access to valuable digital information.

**Automatic crop watering system**

Since 1990, Bertha Ascayo has been the manager of _El Pallar_, a farm in Chacra Province, where fruit trees are grown. In recent years, the climate has become more unpredictable, and the farm has suffered from periods of drought. Bertha is aware that she now needs to manage the watering of her fruit trees more than she has had to in the past.

Bertha will need to make some improvements to the watering system by adding a rain sensor and use a computer program to automate the watering process. She has chosen a company that uses open-source software. The same company also installed the equipment for the watering of the trees.

By using this software, Bertha can program a different watering plan for each type of tree. This includes programming the days of the week, times of the day, and length of the water cycle for each type of tree to be watered. For example, orange trees will be watered on Mondays and Thursdays at 6am for 45 minutes and at 8pm for 30 minutes.

parts.content:
Water is becoming increasingly scarce in Chacra Province, and the provincial government is concerned there will be a point when there is not enough water for all of the farms to water their crops or fruit trees.

To manage water for the watering of crops and fruit trees, the provincial government of Chacra Province intends to use computer modelling to calculate how much water each farm in the province will receive.

Explain **three** factors that will contribute to the accuracy of this computer model.
parts.markscheme:
_Answers may include:_

*   Quality of data input
*   High-quality data will allow for a more accurate model to be developed, as this data will be more reliable / current water levels within each area must be accurate.  
      
    

*   Frequency of readings taken
*   More frequent readings may be able to pick up more subtle changes in the patterns of rainfall to increase the accuracy of the model / taking daily readings and using them to create the model will be more accurate than using monthly readings.  
      
    

*   Number of variables taken into account when developing the model
*   More variables will enable other, potentially less important, factors to be considered when developing the model / what crops are farmed in different areas in the province / the weather conditions in different areas of the province.  
      
    

*   Number of assumptions that have been built into the model
*   If any of the assumptions are weak or false, the model will be inaccurate.  
      
    

*   Quantity of data input
*   If the sample of data is larger, the resulting model will be more accurate / spans several years.

_Award **\[1\]** for identifying a factor that will contribute to the accuracy of the model and **\[1\]** for explanation why that will contribute to the accuracy of the model up to maximum of **\[2\]** marks._

**_OR_**

_Award **\[1\]** for each example given and an additional **\[1\]** if the explanation contributes to the accuracy of the model up to a maximum of **\[2\]** marks._

_  
Mark as **\[2\]** \+ **\[2\]** \+ **\[2\]**._

parts.content:
The farmers of Chacra Province are concerned about the provincial government using a computer model to determine the amount of water they will be allowed to use to water their fruit trees.

To what extent should the farmers of Chacra Province rely on their knowledge and experience, rather than the computer-based solution provided by the provincial government, to manage the watering of the fruit trees?
parts.markscheme:
_Answers may include:_

**Knowledge and experience will:**

*   allow the farmers to see more than water needs for a plant (sick leaves, buds falling, etc.) without needing to develop a model (judgement)
*   be able to include other factors that may arise from time to time, such as quality of the water (e.g., coming with mud), and adjust their watering accordingly (judgement)
*   be able to resolve potential issues that cannot be programmed into the computer or that would be outside the scope of a computational solution (intuition)
*   provide expertise that cannot be quantified / ensure that in a worst-case scenario some of the farmers will be able to grow crops/trees (intuition).

**Use of the provincial model will:**

*   allow water to be distributed evenly between the farmers on a needs basis rather than based on the opinions of one or two stronger characters (equity)
*   potentially have problems, as a scientific approach has limits (e.g., there may be variables that the model does not take into account) – accuracy, the decisions around the water requirements of the trees may be based as much on intuition as algorithms and models
*   potentially have biased model/algorithms influenced by politics, e.g., farmers may not have fully disclosed their data / government may favour certain groups or industries.

**Use of the computer system will:**

*   reduce the amount of time farmers need to spend visibly checking their fields (automation)
*   allow exact water usage to be measured and lead to potential efficiencies in its usage (accuracy)
*   provide quantitative data that could be used as a model for other farmers growing the same trees in similar locations.

_In part (c) of this question it is expected there will be a balance between the terminology related to digital systems and the terminology related to social and ethical impacts._

**Keywords:** agriculture, ecosystems, algorithm, accuracy, reliability, model, modelling, judgement, automation, change, power, systems, ethics, values

questions.specification:
**China's social credit scheme**

  
The Chinese government is proposing a social credit scheme that will reward its citizens with points for what it considers to be good behaviour. This can be done by monitoring citizens’ social media connections, purchasing history and location data.

CCTV cameras are widely used in China, and there are concerns that facial recognition technology that uses artificial intelligence (AI) is already being utilized by the government to detect “good” and “bad” behaviours of its citizens. The facial recognition system requires high-resolution images\*.

* * *

\* high-resolution images: images that are of a quality that allows them to be used for printing. Usually this means they are 300 pixels per inch.

parts.content:
Other governments are considering setting up similar systems that will use facial recognition to gather information about the behaviour of its citizens.

Explain why the developers of the new facial recognition system would need both primary **and** secondary data collection.
parts.markscheme:
Answers may include:

*   Primary data collection, such as collecting potential users interacting with the facial recognition system, would provide information about these interactions in real time. Interviews with developers of similar systems would provide rich and nuanced information.
*   Secondary data collection may involve gathering information about existing facial recognition systems and would provide a wider knowledge base for making informed decisions.
*   Combining the real-time interaction of potential users with the facial recognition system and the data obtained from literature reviews will provide the greatest quantity of information and enable the developers to make informed decisions about the best way to develop the facial recognition system.

_Award **\[1\]** for identifying a reason why_ _the developers of the new facial recognition system would need both primary and secondary data collection_ _and **\[1\]** for each subsequent development of that reason, up to **\[3\]**._

parts.content: Explain why using high-resolution images could be a challenge to the implementation of a facial recognition system.
parts.markscheme:
Answers may include:

*   High resolution images may require a longer transfer time.
*   Which will require computers with high specifications to be processed in a timely manner.
*   May require considerable storage space.
*   May require lossy compression to transfer them quickly which may lead to a reduction in the quality / usefulness of the image.
*   The cost of purchasing the equipment required to obtain high resolution images.

_Award **\[1\]** for identifying a reason why having images with a higher resolution could be a challenge to the implementation of a facial recognition system_ _and **\[1\]** for each subsequent development of that reason up to **\[2\]**._

parts.content: Discuss the advantages **and** disadvantages of a government using a facial recognition system that uses artificial intelligence (AI) to monitor the behaviour of its citizens.
parts.markscheme:
Answers may include:

**Advantages:**

*   May improve people’s behaviour as they will know they are being watched (values, ethics).
*   May stop crime before it happens meaning fewer people in jail or in court (values, ethics, monitoring). Similar to a speeding camera but for people.
*   Might need fewer police to patrol and put themselves in danger (cost).
*   Could help to improve the physical environment by catching those who litter / responsible for polluting the environment.
*   Could allow people to be more accurately identified leading to fewer false accusations/prosecutions (reliability, trust).
*   Allows the government to track and apprehend criminals i.e., makes society safer as criminals are caught (monitoring).
*   AI will be more efficient than manual checking and may learn from its experiences (systems).

**  
Disadvantages:** ·

*   Feeling of being watched will be bad for morale of population (ethics, power, surveillance).
*   May lead to a distrust in the government (trust, values).
*   Might be punished incorrectly (or rewarded incorrectly) if the facial recognition fails (reliability, systems).
*   Criminals might just find other ways of committing crime, such as wearing masks or avoiding the cameras (values).
*   People wear masks (against pollution) or hats which cover their faces (when it is very cold), won’t be recognized that easily (reliability).
*   Invasion of privacy as people are never able to not be under surveillance.
*   May take up considerable resources in developing and maintaining the data and hardware (cost).
*   Facial recognition is not that accurate so there might be many false positives (reliability, systems, accountability).
*   AI may have inherent biases, e.g., it may not recognize particular physical features (values, ethics).

_In part (c) of this question it is expected there will be a balance between the terminology related to digital systems and the terminology related to social and ethical impacts._

**Keywords:** government, data, algorithm, accuracy, reliability, model, monitoring, surveillance, power, systems, ethics, values, trust, accountability, cost, feasibility

parts.content: Explain two limitations of copyright laws
parts.markscheme:
Limitations of copyright laws:

- Copyright laws only protect the expression of ideas, not the ideas themselves. <marks>1 mark</marks>
  - Anyone can use the underlying ideas, concepts, or principles expressed in a copyrighted work.

- Copyright protection is limited in duration. <marks>1 mark</marks>
  - Copyright eventually expires, and works enter the public domain, allowing free use.
  - Example: In most countries, copyright lasts for the life of the author plus 50-70 years.

- Fair use/fair dealing exceptions allow limited use of copyrighted works without permission. <marks>2 marks</marks>
  - Examples: Criticism, commentary, news reporting, research, teaching, etc.
  - The amount used must be reasonable and not harm the market for the work.

- Copyright laws have limitations on enforcement across borders. <marks>1 mark</marks>
  - Copyright is territorial, and laws vary between countries.
  - Enforcing copyright internationally can be challenging.

<note>Award 1 mark for each well-explained limitation, up to a maximum of 6 marks.</note>

parts.content: The evolution of the internet and computing has made it easier for people to illegally copy and distribute works of art. Discuss the impact this is having on the music industry.
parts.markscheme:
Some points could include: 1. Loss of Revenue: Illegal downloading and sharing of music files lead to a reduction in revenue for artists, record labels, and other stakeholders.
With easier access to pirated music, consumers may choose not to pay for songs or albums, directly affecting sales and reducing income for the music industry. 2. Challenges for Emerging Artists: New artists face difficulties in establishing themselves due to the loss of potential revenue streams.
They rely on album sales and streaming royalties to fund their careers, and piracy undermines these opportunities, making it harder to sustain a livelihood solely through music. 3. Shift in Business Models: The industry has had to adapt, with a shift toward streaming services (e.g., Spotify, Apple Music) as a way to counteract piracy.
While these services help reduce illegal downloads, they often provide lower per-stream revenue for artists compared to traditional album sales. 4. Impact on Creativity and Production Quality: Lower revenue may mean fewer resources for high-quality production, as smaller labels and independent artists have less to invest in their music.
This can limit creative exploration and reduce the overall quality and variety of music available to consumers. 5. Increased Reliance on Live Performances and Merchandise: To counterbalance losses from album sales, artists increasingly rely on live performances, tours, and merchandise as primary revenue sources.
However, live performances are not feasible for all artists, especially for those with physical or logistical limitations, meaning they are more severely impacted by illegal distribution. 6. Legal and Ethical Concerns: Piracy raises ethical issues, as it undermines artists' rights to their own work.
The music industry has implemented stricter copyright laws and taken legal action against file-sharing platforms, but enforcement remains challenging due to the global nature of the internet. 7. Potential Positive Exposure: On a positive note, free access to music can increase exposure for some artists, allowing them to reach a wider audience and potentially gain fans who may later pay for concert tickets or official merchandise.
However, this benefit is uncertain and varies widely depending on the artist's popularity and audience. Conclusion: The illegal copying and distribution of music have forced the industry to adapt, with mixed results. While streaming and alternative revenue sources provide some compensation, piracy continues to pose financial and ethical challenges, particularly for smaller artists. The industry must balance between protecting intellectual property and embracing new digital business models that meet changing consumer behaviors. 8 marks Valid points should be accepted with 1 mark for the point and an additional point for a supporting explanation/example.

A conditional is a statement in the algorithm that executes actions based on specific criteria (e.g., if an obstacle is detected, the car stops).

\text{if (obstacle detected):} \\

parts.content: Identify two reasons why variables are critical for self-driving car algorithms.
parts.markscheme:
### Identify two reasons why variables are critical for self-driving car algorithms. <marks>2 marks</marks>

1. Variables are used to store and manipulate data from sensors (e.g., distance to obstacles, speed, location) that is essential for the algorithm to make decisions.

2. Variables allow the algorithm to keep track of the car's state (e.g., current speed, direction) and adjust its behavior accordingly.

<commonMistake>
Failing to recognize that variables are necessary to store and process dynamic data from the car's environment and internal state.
</commonMistake>

parts.content: Explain how loops improve efficiency in a self-driving car's algorithm for real-time monitoring.
parts.markscheme:
<marks>4 marks</marks>

Loops allow continuous monitoring of road conditions by repeatedly checking sensors, enabling quick responses to changes without repeating code manually.

$$
\text{while (car is running):} \\
\quad \text{check sensors} \\
\quad \text{update car state}
$$

parts.content: Discuss the challenges of creating self-driving algorithms without using variables or conditionals.
parts.markscheme:
### Discuss the challenges of creating self-driving algorithms without using variables or conditionals.

Without variables:
- It would be impossible to store and process data from sensors (e.g., distance to obstacles, speed, location) <marks>2 marks</marks>
- The algorithm could not keep track of the car's state (e.g., current speed, direction) or adjust its behavior accordingly <marks>2 marks</marks>

Without conditionals:
- The algorithm would lack the ability to make decisions based on specific criteria or situations <marks>2 marks</marks>
- It could not adapt its behavior based on changing road conditions or obstacles, leading to potential safety issues <marks>2 marks</marks>

parts.content: To what extent does bandwidth availability impact the quality of online activities like streaming and gaming, considering both user experience and network limitations?
parts.markscheme:
- Bandwidth is the maximum amount of data that can be transmitted over a network connection in a given time period <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Higher bandwidth allows for faster data transfer rates and better quality for online activities like:
    - Video streaming (higher resolution, less buffering) <marks>1 mark</marks>
    - Online gaming (lower latency, smoother gameplay) <marks>1 mark</marks>

    - Low bandwidth leads to poor quality video/audio, lag, buffering <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Network limitations that impact bandwidth availability:
    - Type of internet connection (e.g. fiber optic vs DSL) <marks>1 mark</marks>
    - Distance from internet service provider <marks>1 mark</marks>
    - Network congestion during peak usage times <marks>1 mark</marks>
    - Shared bandwidth among multiple users/devices <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Solutions to increase bandwidth:
    - Upgrade to a higher-tier internet plan <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Discuss the ethical implications of user data collection and surveillance on social networks, focusing on privacy, security, and consent.
parts.markscheme:
### Privacy and consent <marks>4 marks</marks>
- User data collection often happens without explicit consent or transparency
- Privacy concerns over personal data being collected, shared, and used for purposes not intended by users
- Lack of control over how personal data is used and disseminated
- Potential for data breaches and unauthorized access to sensitive information

### Security risks <marks>3 marks</marks>
- Collection of user data creates security vulnerabilities and risks of hacking/data breaches
- Centralized data storage makes platforms an attractive target for cyber attacks
- Stolen data can be used for identity theft, fraud, or other malicious activities
$$
\text{Risk} \propto \text{Amount of data collected}
$$

### Surveillance and social control <marks>3 marks</marks>
- User data enables mass surveillance and monitoring of online activities
- Potential for data to be used for social control, manipulation, or discrimination
- Chilling effects on free speech and expression due to perceived monitoring
- Asymmetry of power between platforms with user data and individual users

### Ethical considerations <marks>2 marks</marks>
- Lack of transparency and informed consent violates ethical principles
- User autonomy and right to privacy are undermined
- Utilitarian arguments about benefits must be weighed against ethical costs
- Need for stronger data protection laws and user-centric data governance models

questions.specification:
**Technology disruption in Orams Academy**

Many schools use virtual learning environments (VLEs), such as Firefly, PowerSchool Learning, Schoology, Final Site LMS, and Google Classroom. The VLE is used for document storage, lesson content, and homework management as well as for communication between the school, students and parents.

In addition to the VLE, collaborative cloud-based online tools, such as Google G Suite and Microsoft Office 365, are used by students and teachers.

Recently, Daniela Stark, the principal at Orams Academy, has become concerned that using only one VLE may not prove to be as beneficial as originally thought. She has heard that some teachers and students are also successfully using social media tools, such as Facebook, Twitter and Instagram, to share their learning. She has also been told that the lack of bandwidth that the school experiences at times can cause problems.

Daniela is considering an alternative approach, allowing teachers and students to choose the most appropriate tools to manage their learning.

parts.content:
The increasing use of cloud-based applications and online collaborative tools has led to Orams Academy introducing an acceptableuse policy for students and teachers.

Explain**three** reasons why Orams Academy decided to introduce an acceptable use policy.
parts.markscheme:
_Answers may include:_

*   To establish a philosophy of Orams Academy relating to the use of online environments…
*   which acts as a guiding statement and provides the overarching principles for online behaviour (values).  
    

*   To establish a code of conduct (values)…
*   that provides clear and unambiguous directions about how staff and students should act while online / using appropriate language in text chats / avoiding bullying behaviour / avoiding copying or plagiarism.  
      
    

*   To outline sanctions…
*   that would be imposed if staff or students did not adhere to the code of conduct (values).  
      
    

*   To establish a disclaimer…
*   so that Orams Academy is not responsible for the actions of the staff or students if an illegal activity is carried out / managers at Orams Academy are able to supervise everything that is posted onto the VLE / managers at Orams Academy would be able to read students’ messages (spaces).  
      
    

*   To establish data privacy and protection guidelines (security)…
*   so it will be transparent who owns the data created in the VLE, for how long, and what type of data is collected by the VLE servers (systems).

_Award **\[1\]** for each reason given and an additional **\[1\]** for a development of this reason up to a maximum of **\[2\]** marks._

_Mark as **\[2\]** \+ **\[2\]** \+ **\[2\]**._

_In part (c) of this question it is expected there will be a balance between the terminology related to digital systems and the terminology related to social and ethical impacts._

**Keywords:** cloud, policy, security, ubiquity, digital media, remote learning, change, spaces, systems, values

parts.content: Discuss whether Daniela should make every teacher at Orams Academy use the same learning platform **or** allow each teacher to choose their own preferred learning management approach.
parts.markscheme:
_Answers may include:_

**A single learning platform:**

*   This means that all of the information is centralized and can be easily accessed by staff, students and parents (systems).
*   The training requirements for staff are reduced, as they only have to use one system (systems).
*   It prevents the use of a range of formats that may be incompatible, meaning that resources cannot be shared, or may need adapting to work on a different platform (systems).
*   Having a common learning platform would make it convenient for the IT department to take backup/restore and roll over data to new academic year (systems).
*   It makes it easier for senior managers to monitor the actions of staff, view lessons, etc., for consistency (values, power).
*   Can be integrated into any acceptable use policy or training requirements (values).
*   May be costly and based on proprietary software, meaning that fixes could be expensive and take a long time to happen (change).
*   If the single platform is inaccessible for any reason, all learning content would be inaccessible (systems).

**Teachers choosing their own preferred learning management approach:**

*   allows teachers to use their expertise of particular tools
*   allows teachers to feel they are being allowed to use their creativity and innovative approach to the teaching and learning (power)
*   may integrate with social media tools that may be blocked by the school and provide a better user experience
*   may provide better functionality than a “one size fits all” approach
*   may lead to staff or students working outside an acceptable use policy / using the sites inappropriately
*   the training requirements for staff will be increased if the staff are unfamiliar with the platforms / training will only occur on an _ad hoc_ basis / training might be decreased since staff are already familiar with the tools they choose to use
*   expertise of a platform may depend on particular teachers – if they leave, then no one may be able to support students with this tool (systems).

**Keywords:** business, learning, education, training, judgement, platform, monitoring, acceptable use policy, interoperability, policy, change, systems, values

questions.specification:
### Source A: Mainframe Computers in Large-Scale Data Processing
Mainframe computers are built to handle high volumes of data and support thousands of simultaneous users. Organizations like banks and governments use them for transactions, data storage, and large-scale processing.

For example, a bank uses mainframes to process real-time transactions, ensuring accurate and fast services for millions of customers worldwide.


### Source B: The Role of Operating Systems and Applications in Personal Computers
Operating systems like Windows, macOS, and Linux act as the interface between a computer’s hardware and the user. They enable applications—such as word processors, browsers, and gaming software—to run smoothly. The OS manages resources, facilitating multiple applications to work together.

For instance, Windows provides a platform for applications like Microsoft Office and Adobe Photoshop to function efficiently for diverse user needs.

![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/ff2cf25c-433d-47a2-bbad-e28c349ad345)


### Source C: Quantum Computing: The Future of Processing Power
Quantum computing is an emerging area that leverages quantum bits (qubits) to solve problems much faster than classical computers. Quantum computers hold potential for breakthroughs in fields like cryptography and artificial intelligence by handling complex calculations in seconds.

For instance, pharmaceutical companies use quantum simulations to predict molecular interactions, significantly accelerating drug discovery.

### Source D: Input and Output Devices in Modern Computing
Input devices such as keyboards, touchscreens, and voice recognition systems allow users to interact with computers, while output devices like monitors, printers, and VR headsets present information. Input-output functionality enables usability across personal and professional applications.

For example, a virtual reality (VR) system uses motion sensors as input and a VR headset as output, creating an immersive experience for the user.


![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/a58f32c1-c8e5-4011-8519-c052b2e03df2)

parts.content: With reference to Sources A–D and your own knowledge, analyze how advancements in different types of computing have influenced both personal and professional applications. Include examples from at least two different computing types.
parts.markscheme:
### Introduction

Advancements in different types of computing, such as mainframes, quantum computing, operating systems, and wearable technology, have had a profound influence on both professional and personal applications. <marks>1 mark</marks>

### Professional Applications

#### Mainframe Computers
Mainframe computers are essential in sectors like finance and government for handling data-intensive tasks. Examples include:

- Banking: Processing large-scale, real-time transactions to serve millions of users securely. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Government: Supporting massive databases for public records, enabling efficient data management and retrieval. <marks>1 mark</marks>

#### Quantum Computing
Quantum computing is an emerging technology with transformative potential in research-driven fields. Examples include:

- Drug Discovery: Simulating molecular interactions to speed up the development of new treatments. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Cryptography: Enhancing data security and solving complex encryption problems quickly, which is crucial for protecting sensitive information. <marks>1 mark</marks>

### Personal Applications

#### Operating Systems on Personal Devices
Operating systems on personal devices are the foundation of modern personal computing, enhancing user experience through:

- Multitasking: Enabling seamless switching between applications on smartphones and computers. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Accessibility: Intuitive interfaces (e.g., touchscreens) that make computing accessible to diverse user groups. <marks>1 mark</marks>

#### Wearable Technology
Wearable technology, such as fitness trackers and smartwatches, supports health monitoring and connectivity. <marks>1 mark</marks>

### Conclusion

In summary, these advancements allow professionals to handle complex, data-heavy tasks efficiently and provide users with accessible, responsive technology in daily life. <marks>1 mark</marks>

questions.specification:
**Source A**

![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/68fc2d2e-593d-4a63-83eb-d194006f2ecf)

**Source B (IslaVerde self-description excerpt)**

IslaVerde’s clinic relies on video consultations with specialists on the mainland. The clinic’s policy document lists minimum network requirements: stable upload speed for video, low packet loss for medical images, and encrypted connections for patient confidentiality. The clinic currently uses fibre as its primary connection and keeps a satellite link as backup for outages. Staff report that when fibre fails, the satellite link keeps messaging and record access running, but video quality often drops and consultations take longer. The policy notes that home patients often connect via mobile hotspots, which can be affected by congestion and data caps. The clinic wants to expand remote monitoring (wearables sending daily measurements), but worries that inconsistent connectivity will create unequal care and that more connected devices will increase cybersecurity risks.

**Source C (data usage statistics from IslaVerde)**  

Over 12 months, fibre uptime averaged 99.1%; there were 6 fibre outages, including one lasting 11 hours.

During fibre outages, satellite enabled basic services, but average consultation duration increased from 18 minutes to 31 minutes.

Mobile hotspot use for home consultations increased by 44%; dropout rates are 3× higher on mobile than on fibre.

Security log summary: 23 blocked intrusion attempts on the clinic gateway; 0 confirmed breaches, but 2 incidents involved staff reusing weak passwords on remote access.

Patient survey: 26% report postponing at least one appointment due to “internet problems,” concentrated in outer hamlets.

**Source D (published letter to IslaVerde)**

The clinic’s connectivity plan treats the internet like a neutral utility: if you have “a link,” you have care. But networks are not interchangeable pipes. Latency, congestion, data caps, and outages shape who can be seen by a doctor and when—and they shape how stressful that care becomes. Pushing remote monitoring sounds modern, yet it expands surveillance-like data collection and widens the gap between households with stable connections and those relying on fragile mobile coverage. Security is also social, not just technical. A single reused password can undermine expensive infrastructure, especially when staff are pressured to “make it work” during outages. IslaVerde should be cautious about building critical health services on connectivity that residents do not control. Resilience is not only about backup links; it is also about maintaining non-digital alternatives and ensuring that network inequality does not become health inequality.

parts.content: Suggest two ways the clinic could reduce inequality in access to telemedicine, with reference to Source B.
parts.markscheme:
**Suggest (Total <marks>4 marks</marks>)**


**Answer points**


* Provide clinic-based telehealth rooms or kiosks with stable connections in outer hamlets to reduce reliance on congested mobile hotspots and data caps described in Source B. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Subsidise or loan fixed wireless or fibre last‑mile connections, or partner with ISPs to zero-rate telemedicine data to address data caps and unstable uploads noted in Source B. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Offer asynchronous options such as store-and-forward images or messages when video fails, reducing exclusion during congestion and unstable connections highlighted in Source B. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Provide technical support, simple connection guidance, or schedule appointments in lower-congestion periods to mitigate mobile congestion and variable home connectivity identified in Source B. <marks>1 mark</marks>


**Awarding marks**


Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for a clear method to reduce inequality.  

Award an additional <marks>1 mark</marks> for explicit reference to Source B constraints such as congestion, data caps, unstable uploads, or minimum requirements for each suggestion, up to a maximum of <marks>4 marks</marks>.


**Marking guidance**


<note>Each suggestion must clearly link to the inequality issues in Source B, specifically mobile congestion, data caps, or variable home connectivity. Suggestions without reference to these constraints should receive limited credit.</note>


**Sample answer**


The clinic could provide telehealth rooms or kiosks in outer hamlets with stable wired connections, reducing reliance on mobile hotspots that suffer congestion and data caps.  

It could also offer asynchronous consultation options such as store-and-forward messages or images so patients are not excluded when video calls fail due to unstable uploads or peak-time congestion.

parts.content: Compare and contrast what Source C and Source D indicate about the benefits and risks of relying on networks for healthcare.
parts.markscheme:
**Compare and contrast (Total <marks>6 marks</marks>)**


**Answer points**


* Source C quantifies reliability and impact, reporting 99.1 percent uptime, six fibre outages, longer consultations on satellite, and higher dropouts on mobile, while Source D explains why networks differ by identifying latency, congestion, data caps, and outages as factors shaping access to care. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Source C presents cybersecurity data including 23 blocked intrusion attempts and two password reuse incidents, while Source D stresses that security is socio-technical and that pressure during outages leads to weaker practices. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Both sources highlight benefits such as services continuing through backup links and expanded access to remote healthcare. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Both sources warn about inequality, with Source C noting appointment postponements concentrated in outer hamlets and Source D arguing that network inequality becomes health inequality. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Source C provides statistical evidence of performance impacts, while Source D offers a critical perspective on the limits of treating networks as neutral utilities. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Source C shows concrete performance differences between fibre, satellite, and mobile connections, while Source D argues that networks are not interchangeable pipes and affect who can access care. <marks>1 mark</marks>


**Awarding marks**


Award up to <marks>6 marks</marks> for points that clearly compare and contrast the two sources.  
Award <marks>1 mark</marks> per relevant similarity or difference.


**Marking guidance**


<note>Responses focused on only one source can receive a maximum of <marks>2 marks</marks>. To reach full marks, responses must address both sources and identify both similarities and differences. Points that describe one source without linking to the other should receive limited credit.</note>


**Sample answer**


Source C and Source D address similar issues around digital healthcare but from different perspectives. Source C focuses on measurable reliability and impact, reporting high fibre uptime, a small number of outages, longer consultation times on satellite connections, and higher dropout rates on mobile networks.  

In contrast, Source D explains why these differences exist by highlighting factors such as latency, congestion, data caps, and outages that shape who can access care. Both sources recognise benefits including continuity of services through backup links and expanded access to remote care. However, they also warn about inequality. Source C shows postponements concentrated in outer hamlets, while Source D argues more broadly that unequal networks translate into health inequality. Overall, Source C provides statistical evidence of performance differences, whereas Source D offers a critical interpretation of networks as non-neutral systems that affect access differently.

parts.content: To what extent should IslaVerde expand internet-dependent healthcare services (such as video consultations and remote monitoring)? Answer this with reference to all the sources (A–D) and your own knowledge of the Digital Society course. Consider how networks enable and constrain participation, reliability and resilience, cybersecurity and privacy, and the ways connectivity can create new digital divides.
parts.markscheme:
**To what extent (Total <marks>12 marks</marks>)**


**Answer points**


* Networks enable healthcare by supporting video consultations that expand specialist access to remote patients. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Source B shows that fibre uptime of 99.1 percent supports the feasibility of internet-dependent healthcare. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Outages and degraded satellite performance limit equivalence of care, as consultation times increase from 18 to 31 minutes during fibre failures. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Mobile dropout rates three times higher than fibre and postponements affecting 26 percent of patients demonstrate a digital divide in access. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Source D argues that latency, congestion, data caps, and outages make networks not interchangeable, shaping who can access care. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Backup links provide resilience but require service design for degraded modes such as asynchronous alternatives. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Source D notes that resilience should also include maintaining non-digital alternatives rather than relying solely on backup links. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Encryption supports confidentiality, but Source C shows ongoing cybersecurity risks through 23 blocked intrusion attempts. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Password reuse incidents illustrate social factors where staff pressure undermines technical security measures. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Remote monitoring through wearables expands care but introduces surveillance-like data collection and privacy concerns. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Network inequality widens gaps between households with stable connections and those dependent on mobile coverage. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Expansion should proceed cautiously with safeguards including offline alternatives, equitable access plans, authentication training, and privacy governance. <marks>1 mark</marks>


**Awarding marks**


Award up to <marks>12 marks</marks> for a balanced evaluative judgement that considers enablement, constraints, resilience, cybersecurity, privacy, and digital divides.  
Award <marks>1 mark</marks> per relevant evaluative point grounded in the sources and course knowledge.


**Marking guidance**


<note>Responses using only source material or only own knowledge can receive a maximum of <marks>8 marks</marks>. Full marks require synthesis of sources with relevant own knowledge and a balanced judgement on expanding internet-dependent healthcare.</note>


**Sample answer**


Internet-dependent healthcare on IslaVerde should be expanded, but only with appropriate safeguards in place. Networks enable healthcare by allowing video consultations that connect remote patients with specialists. Source B shows that high fibre uptime supports the feasibility of these services under normal conditions.  

However, constraints limit equivalence of care. Source C shows that outages and reliance on satellite links increase consultation times, while mobile connections suffer much higher dropout rates and lead to postponed appointments for many patients. Source D reinforces this by arguing that networks are not interchangeable, as latency, congestion, and data caps shape who can realistically access care.  

Backup links improve resilience, but Source D emphasises that resilience should also include non-digital alternatives. Cybersecurity and privacy concerns remain significant. Although encryption is used, Source C reports blocked intrusion attempts and password reuse, showing that security is partly social. Remote monitoring may expand care but also raises surveillance and privacy concerns.  

Overall, expanding digital healthcare can improve access, but without offline alternatives, equitable access planning, staff training, and strong privacy governance, it risks deepening existing health inequalities alongside digital divides.

questions.specification:
**Social media and political tension**

National governments sometimes block websites at a time of political tension. One example was in 2011, when a government prevented its citizens’ access to _Twitter_, _Facebook_ and _YouTube_. Images on these websites were considered inappropriate, with the potential to cause hatred, violence and political unrest in that country. The national government gave direct orders to the internet service providers (ISPs) to block access to these sites for four hours. The aim was to give enough time for site officials to remove the offending images from their websites.

Despite these attempts by the government, some citizens were still able to access these images using proxy servers.

Many people, such as academics, believe that this approach is not appropriate and that governments should focus on the education of their citizens so that they are able to make informed decisions about how to react to potentially offensive information on websites and social media.
